text: This is a seventy three year old male who presented with right hypochondrial pain for ten days and loss of appetite. He was evaluated with the triple phase CT which showed a twelve centimeter tumor mainly involving segment six and seven also compressing segment five and eight.
This is the arterial phase showing enhancement and the washout on the venous phase.
The video shows the tumor predominantly supplied by the right posterior pedicle, with a large inferior right hepatic vein visibly draining the right lobe of the liver.
The video displays the port positions for the robotic da Vinci SI system, which are strategically placed for optimal access during the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to divide the falciform ligament up to the hepatic veins, initiating the dissection of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses scissors in the robotic arm one and cadier forceps in arm two, while the falciform ligament is retracted with an endo loop.
The surgeon begins the hilar dissection using a combination of Maryland bipolar forceps and monopolar curved scissors. The right hepatic artery is carefully looped during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the right portal vein and applies a silk tape around it for isolation.
The surgeon clips the right hepatic artery and the right portal vein using a hemoloclip applicator, securing these vessels within the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the right portal vein, with visible color demarcation appearing on the liver surface. Parenchymal surface marking is performed to delineate the area for resection.
The surgeon utilizes robotic firefly technology to visually highlight the line of demarcation on the liver surface, enhancing the contrast between different tissue areas.

The surgeon uses a rubber band stitch to retract the liver on both sides. Initial surface parenchymal transection is performed using ultrasonic shears and Maryland bipolar forceps on the robotic arm two.

The assistant surgeon performs suctioning and deeper parenchymal transection using the CUSA device, while the console surgeon continues parenchymal transection with a combination of bipolar and ultrasonic shears.
With rubber band traction applied on both sides of the liver, the surgeon creates sufficient tension to open the liver and perform parenchymal transection.
The assistant surgeon applies the QSA device to the liver tissue. The Segment V vein, which drains into the middle hepatic vein, is identified, clipped, and divided.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis in the liver tissue using a combination of bipolar and ultrasonic shears, ensuring controlled bleeding during the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs hilar plate dissection, dividing the plate intraparenchymally. The robotic Firefly system with ICG is utilized to visually confirm the bile duct division.
The surgeon loops and divides the large inferior right hepatic vein using an endo-GIA stapler. The segment eight vein is isolated, clipped with hemolog and metal clips, and then divided.
The surgeon divides the right hepatic vein using an endo-GIA stapler, completing the transection of this major vessel.

The surgeon releases all lateral attachments to the right lobe of the liver, including diaphragmatic attachments of the tumor to the posterior superior aspect. Robotic instruments provide stable traction, facilitating dissection in these difficult areas.
The surgeon lifts the right lobe of the liver from the retroperitoneum, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon divides the hepatocaval ligament, also known as Makuchi's ligament, using an endo-GIA stapler, completing the parenchymal transection of the liver.
The specimen is displayed, showing a large ten-centimeter tumor that was removed during the hepatectomy procedure.
